rationale.R1.1: The artifact's foundational conceptual move — equating predictive skill with inter-model agreement (spread inversion) rather than accuracy against observations — is itself the primary claim to interrogate; the thesis stands or falls on whether this redefinition is defensible.
rationale.R1.2: Multiple load-bearing logical failures are present: (1) Northeast Brazil and Southeast Australia are post-hoc excluded from headline statistics on the grounds that their divergence 'reflects known structural uncertainty,' making the 'all six regions' framing circular; (2) the inference from low inter-model spread to 'well-constrained physical processes' is unwarranted without out-of-sample validation; (3) the conclusion that planners can act 'with confidence' follows from agreement, not accuracy.
rationale.R1.3: The headline claim — 'high predictive skill across all six regions' — is directly contradicted by the body: two of six regions (Northeast Brazil 0.41, Southeast Australia 0.48) fall well below the reported mean of 0.78 and exhibit sign disagreement. Their exclusion from summary statistics while remaining 'part of the evaluated panel' makes the evidence-claim gap load-bearing.
